author: Dr. Sarah Beckman
content:
INPATIENT DISCHARGE SUMMARY — AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE — 2/11/2027, 4:30 PM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
Admission date: 2/8/2027
Discharge date: 2/11/2027 (Hospital Day 4)
Discharge type: AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE (AMA)
Interpreter: Spanish (Language Line) used throughout.

ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS:
Active SLE with new inflammatory myositis and oropharyngeal dysphagia; severe protein-calorie malnutrition.

BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE:
Ms. Sandoval was admitted from the Hopkins ED on 2/8/2027 with proximal muscle weakness, dysphagia, weight loss, low albumin (2.3), and CK 1840 in the setting of known active SLE. She was admitted to general medicine with consults to rheumatology, neurology, GI, nutrition, ID, and dermatology.

Day 1-2 (2/8-2/9): Maintained NPO. Aspiration precautions. IV fluids titrated cautiously. K and Mg repleted. Modified barium swallow study performed 2/9 — showed mild oropharyngeal dysphagia with delayed swallow initiation and small amount of laryngeal penetration with thin liquids; no overt aspiration; recommended nectar-thick liquids and pureed diet with chin-tuck precautions, NG tube placement deferred pending biopsy results. EMG was scheduled for 2/12 (HD 5) — not performed prior to AMA discharge. Muscle biopsy of left vastus lateralis was scheduled for 2/13 — not performed. Skin biopsy of elbow scaling lesion was performed 2/10 — preliminary read pending at time of AMA discharge.

Dermatology consult (2/9): Examined the scaling lesions on elbows, knees, dorsum of hands. Considered dermatomyositis (Gottron's papules), psoriasis vs. SLE-related discoid, and noted the unusual pattern of cutaneous changes with the patient's nutritional state raised the question of nutritional dermatosis (kwashiorkor-associated). Recommended biopsy (performed 2/10).

Neurology consult (2/9): Documented bilateral proximal weakness (3+/5 hip flexion and shoulder abduction). Recommended EMG/NCS prior to muscle biopsy. Suggested MRI thigh muscles to identify a target site for biopsy if EMG nondiagnostic.

Nutrition consult (2/8): Calculated estimated needs at 1100-1300 kcal/d initial (avoiding refeeding syndrome given severe malnutrition and electrolyte derangements on admission). Recommended slow advancement with thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE any carbohydrate-containing intake. Patient started on 800 kcal/d via pureed diet on 2/10, with thiamine 100 mg IV daily x 5 days started. Daily phosphorus, magnesium, and potassium monitoring instituted.

ID consult (2/9): Recommended hold on further immunosuppression escalation until pre-immunosuppression screening complete. Drew TB-IGRA (negative reported 2/10), HBV/HCV (negative), HIV (non-reactive), Strongyloides serology (negative), CMV PCR (negative), EBV PCR (negative). With this clean screen, the team was preparing to discuss with rheumatology whether to add pulse methylprednisolone after confirming biopsy direction.

Rheumatology consult (2/9, 2/10): Multiple recommendations including continuing HCQ, continuing prednisone 30 mg daily until biopsy direction guides further therapy, holding MMF temporarily while infection screen returned, planning to add azathioprine OR pulse methylprednisolone after diagnostic clarity. Strongly advocated for completing the diagnostic workup (EMG, muscle biopsy, skin biopsy results) BEFORE further therapy changes. Strongly advised against discharge.

MENTAL STATUS / SOCIAL CONCERNS DURING ADMISSION:
Patient and family expressed significant distress over the financial implications of the hospitalization. They were informed multiple times by social work that all current Hopkins inpatient care was being delivered under emergency Medicaid coverage triggered by the admission (a different mechanism than outpatient Medicaid) and that biopsies and additional diagnostics would not incur out-of-pocket charges during the admission. Patient/family verbally acknowledged understanding but persistent anxiety remained.

On the morning of 2/11, the patient stated she wanted to go home. She gave several reasons: 'I am scared of what they will do to my leg' (referring to the planned muscle biopsy); 'My mother needs me — my brother is alone'; 'I can do this at home'; and persistent worry about future bills. Despite extensive discussion with the medicine team (Dr. Beckman, Dr. Castro), rheumatology (Dr. Tanaka and Dr. Park), social work (Diana Mercado, LCSW, who came in specifically for this conversation), and a Spanish-speaking ethics consultant (Dr. Rivera), the patient maintained her decision.

At the time of AMA discharge:
- Patient retained capacity to make decisions per ethics consult.
- Family (mother, aunt) were ambivalent but ultimately supported patient's decision.
- AMA form completed in Spanish and signed by patient.
- Risks of AMA were explained in detail: progression of myositis, worsening dysphagia with aspiration pneumonia risk, possible respiratory failure, progressive malnutrition, sepsis from immunosuppressed state with new infection. Patient verbalized understanding.
- Outpatient follow-up plan made (see below) and patient agreed to follow it.

DISCHARGE EXAM:
Vitals 12:15 PM: BP 100/62, HR 102, RR 18, T 98.4 F, SpO2 96% RA. Weight 41.8 kg (slight improvement from 41.2 at admission, modest fluid + nutrition).
General: Cachectic young woman, still appears ill but stable.
MSK: Proximal weakness unchanged from admission (3+/5 hip flexion and shoulder abduction).
Skin: Scaling lesions on elbows and knees unchanged. Skin biopsy site (left elbow) bandaged.
Swallow: Tolerating pureed diet with chin-tuck precautions. No witnessed aspiration during admission.
Labs (2/11 AM): Hgb 9.1 (slightly down from 9.4); Albumin 2.5 (improving from 2.3); CK 1620 (slight improvement); K 4.0, Mg 1.9 (both repleted, normal); Phos 3.1.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS (sent home with):
- Prednisone 30 mg PO daily (continued)
- Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO BID (continued)
- MMF 1 g PO BID — patient given 30-day supply through hospital charity
- Levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily
- Calcium 500 mg BID, Vitamin D 1000 IU daily
- Thiamine 100 mg PO daily x 14 more days
- Multivitamin daily
- Omeprazole 20 mg daily
- TrimethSulf 80/400 mg M/W/F for PCP prophylaxis (NEW — given immunosuppression)
- Nystatin oral suspension PRN for thrush prophylaxis
- Ensure Plus 2 bottles BID for caloric supplementation (samples provided)

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
- Pureed diet with nectar-thick liquids, chin-tuck precautions (in Spanish, with diagrams).
- Small frequent meals. Take time eating.
- Use straw for liquids if helpful.
- Daily weights at home (provided digital scale through charity program).
- Return immediately for: choking, food sticking, coughing during meals, shortness of breath, fever, weakness worsening, fall, vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, severe headache, vision change, dark urine, leg swelling, oral or genital ulcers, chest pain.

DISCHARGE FOLLOW-UP:
- Hopkins rheumatology: 2/15/2027 (4 days) — already booked; charity grant secured.
- Esperanza Health (Dr. Williams, PCP): 2/16/2027 — booked.
- Hopkins outpatient labs 2/14/2027: CBC, CMP, CK, ESR, CRP, anti-dsDNA, C3/C4, UPC.
- Speech/swallow re-evaluation outpatient: 2/19/2027 — booked.
- EMG and muscle biopsy: REQUIRED — outpatient appointment 2/22/2027 — booked, financial-aid approved.
- Skin biopsy results: will be communicated to outpatient rheumatology by 2/16/2027.

The family was given multiple emergency contact options, the social worker's direct cell phone, and explicit instructions to return immediately if anything worsens.

This is documented as an AMA discharge. The patient and family were repeatedly informed that this is a high-risk decision. They acknowledged understanding.
attestation:
Electronically signed by: Dr. Sarah Beckman, MD (attending), Dr. Maria Castro, MD (resident)
Internal Medicine, Johns Hopkins Hospital
Date: 2/11/2027
